ground cobalt-blue glass; used in European painting from 15th-18th c.; made by fusing cobalt oxide with potash and silica, then grinding; coarser grades produce deeper blue; historical pigment
calcium copper silicate (cuprorivaite); oldest known synthetic pigment, produced from ~3100 BCE; made by heating quartz sand, lime, copper, and natron to ~900°C; historical pigment
copper-based frit related to Egyptian Blue; produced at lower firing temperatures; found in ancient Egyptian tomb paintings and Roman frescoes; historical pigment
lead stannate (Type I); primary bright yellow in European painting from ~1300-1750; identified in works by Vermeer, Titian, and Rembrandt; lost and rediscovered in 1941; historical pigment
lead tin oxide (Type II); warmer and more opaque than Type I; contains free tin oxide; found primarily in Italian and Northern European painting; historical pigment
variant of lead tin yellow with higher tin content; deeper golden tone used as a warm yellow in Renaissance painting; historical pigment
lead antimonate; one of the oldest synthetic pigments, found in Babylonian tiles from 500 BCE; widely used in European oil painting from 17th c. onward; historical pigment
lead-tin oxide fired at high temperature; the orange variant produced by altering the lead-to-tin ratio and kiln conditions; historical pigment
historical term for a yellow lake pigment precipitated onto a chalk or alum base; Renaissance 'pink' referred to a pale yellow-green, not the modern hue; historical pigment
deeper variant of the historical pink lake; the shift from yellow to rose occurred as the term migrated from Dutch 'pinck' (a yellow dye lake) to the modern color meaning; historical pigment
recreation of a medieval blue pigment described by Emil Ernst Ploss; copper-based pigment produced from basic copper carbonate; historical pigment
synthetic basic copper carbonate; historically produced by pouring a copper solution over chalk; used as a cheaper alternative to azurite from the 17th c.; historical pigment
refined grade of blue verditer or azurite; 'bice' from Italian 'bigio' (grey-blue); a standard house-painter's blue in 17th-18th c. England; historical pigment
basic copper carbonate mineral; the most important blue pigment in European painting before ultramarine; mined in Hungary, Germany, and France; historical pigment
rare chromium-rich metamorphic rock found only in Tawmaw, Myanmar; vivid green from kosmochlor (a chromium-bearing pyroxene); historical pigment
basic copper carbonate mineral; one of the oldest known green pigments; used in Egyptian tomb paintings from 3000 BCE; the green companion to azurite; historical pigment
hydrated copper phyllosilicate; soft blue-green mineral associated with copper deposits; used as a pigment in antiquity, mentioned by Theophrastus and Pliny; historical pigment
pale blue-green mineral pigment ground from turquoise (hydrated copper aluminum phosphate); prized across Persian, Egyptian, and Mesoamerican cultures; historical pigment
copper chloride hydroxide mineral; named for the Atacama Desert, Chile; a green pigment found on ancient Chinese bronzes and in Egyptian artifacts; historical pigment
hydrated iron phosphate mineral; forms deep blue-black crystals; found in waterlogged archaeological contexts; darkens dramatically on exposure to light; historical pigment
sodium aluminum silicate chloride; a feldspathoid mineral; the pale blue-grey matrix stone often found alongside lapis lazuli; historical pigment
rare blue-violet silicate mineral found primarily in the Pyrenees; identified as the blue pigment in Catalan Romanesque murals (11th-12th c.); historical pigment
aluminum silicate mineral; name from Greek 'kyanos' (blue); variable hardness along different crystal axes; used as a refractory mineral; historical pigment
calcium vanadium silicate; vivid blue-green mineral first described in 1967 in Oregon; named for its calcium, vanadium, and silicon content; historical pigment
lazurite in a matrix of calcite, sodalite, and pyrite; source of natural ultramarine; mined in Badakhshan, Afghanistan since the 6th millennium BCE; historical pigment
the grey residue from lapis lazuli extraction after the finest blue has been drawn out; a cool neutral used as an underpainting grey in Italian Renaissance workshops; historical pigment
mercury sulfide (HgS); the principal ore of mercury; used as a brilliant red pigment since antiquity; found in Chinese lacquerware from the Shang dynasty (~1500 BCE); historical pigment
arsenic trisulfide; intensely toxic golden-yellow pigment; name from Latin 'auripigmentum' (gold pigment); used in Egyptian, Persian, and medieval European painting; historical pigment
arsenic sulfide; the orange-red companion to orpiment; photosensitive - degrades to yellow pararealgar on light exposure; used in Chinese and Persian manuscript illumination; historical pigment
calcium copper arsenate hydroxide mineral; pale yellow-green secondary mineral found in oxidized copper-arsenic deposits; historical pigment
lead sulfide (PbS); the principal ore of lead; metallic grey mineral used as kohl eyeliner in ancient Egypt from the pre-Dynastic period (~3000 BCE); historical pigment
copper iron sulfide; the most important copper ore mineral; brassy golden color that tarnishes to iridescent blues and purples; historical pigment
copper sulfide; named after Italian mineralogist Niccolo Covelli; indigo-blue mineral with a distinctive iridescent tarnish; historical pigment
copper sulfide; important copper ore; dark grey to black mineral found in supergene enrichment zones of copper deposits; historical pigment
copper iron sulfide mineral (Cu5FeS4); known as peacock ore for its iridescent purple-blue tarnish; an important copper ore since the Bronze Age; historical pigment
iron sulfide (FeS2); fool's gold; the most common sulfide mineral; its name from Greek 'pyr' (fire) for the sparks it produces when struck; used historically in fire-starting kits; historical pigment
toxic metalloid element known since antiquity; used in kohl eyeliner as stibnite (Sb2S3) in ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia; alchemical symbol for the wild spirit of nature; historical pigment
manganese iron phosphate mineral; named for its purple color; forms by oxidation of lithiophilite in pegmatites; first described in 1905; historical pigment
pigment ground from meteorite material; extraterrestrial iron-nickel alloy; one of the rarest pigment sources — literally not of this earth; historical pigment
celadonite-rich clay from the Verona region of Italy; the classic terre verte; prized since Roman times as an underpainting green for flesh tones in fresco and tempera; historical pigment
green earth (terre verte) from Bavarian deposits; a glauconite-celadonite clay; cooler and greyer than the Veronese variety; historical pigment
green earth from Russian deposits; typically richer in celadonite than Western European sources; a bluer, more saturated terre verte; historical pigment
rare chromium-bearing clay mineral; an exceptionally permanent green earth; named after Prince Volkonsky; found almost exclusively in the Ural Mountains of Russia; historical pigment
sodium iron silicate mineral; named for Aegir, the Norse god of the sea; dark green to black prismatic crystals found in alkaline igneous rocks; historical pigment
calcium aluminum iron silicate; name from Greek 'epidosis' (increase) for the elongated crystal form; pistachio to olive green; found in metamorphic and igneous rocks worldwide; historical pigment
green earth pigment sourced from deposits near Florence; a warm-toned terre verte used extensively in Tuscan fresco painting; historical pigment
serpentinite stone from Andeer in the Swiss Alps; a pale sage green metamorphic rock used historically in Alpine architecture and as a pigment; historical pigment
microcrystalline quartz colored green by iron silicates or chlorite; used as an ornamental stone since antiquity; found in riverbeds and volcanic formations worldwide; historical pigment
quartz colored by inclusions of chlorite, actinolite, or fuchsite; a pale sage-green stone ground as a pigment; historical pigment
opalescent silica mineral with green body color from trace nickel or iron; ground as a pale olive-green pigment; historical pigment

porphyritic igneous rock with green feldspar and pyroxene crystals; 'porfido verde antico' was quarried in Sparta and prized throughout the Roman Empire for imperial columns and sarcophagi; historical pigment
natural iron oxyhydroxide (goethite) in clay; the most fundamental and universal pigment in human history; found in cave paintings from Lascaux (~17,000 BCE) to modern palettes; historical pigment
anhydrous iron oxide (hematite) in clay; used in burial rituals from ~100,000 BCE; the oldest pigment in continuous human use; found in every inhabited continent; historical pigment
iron oxide earth with mixed goethite and hematite; a warm dark earth tone; one of the workhorse pigments of European easel painting; historical pigment
dark iron-rich clay earth; a natural black-brown pigment from mineral-rich soil deposits; used as a ground tone in fresco and panel painting; historical pigment
historical Italian pigment; a warm grey-brown earth from Italian deposits; the name suggests Berlin black but the color is a muted stone grey; historical pigment
white calcium carbonate earth from the Albula region of the Swiss Alps; a natural chalk white with warm undertone; historical pigment
crystalline calcium magnesium carbonate; a sparkling white mineral ground as a pigment and extender; named for the French geologist Deodat de Dolomieu; historical pigment
iron-rich microcrystalline quartz; deep brick-red; one of the most widely used ornamental stones in antiquity; Egyptian scarabs and Minoan seals were carved from it; historical pigment
silica mineral with red body color from iron oxide inclusions; a warm orange-red stone pigment; historical pigment
pale translucent chalcedony; the lightest grade of carnelian; prized in ancient Mesopotamia, Egypt, and the Indus Valley for cylinder seals and jewelry; historical pigment
corundum mineral colored pink by trace chromium; ground as a delicate rose-grey pigment; historical pigment
pink variety of zoisite colored by manganese; named for Thule, the mythical northern land; first discovered in Norway in 1820; historical pigment
manganese carbonate mineral; name from Greek 'rhodon' (rose) and 'chroma' (color); the national mineral of Argentina; raspberry pink banded crystals; historical pigment
mineral pigment sourced from the French Riviera coast; a muted mauve-grey stone ground as a pigment; historical pigment
green-grey metamorphic slate; a fine-grained mudstone pigment; slate has been used as a writing and painting surface since Roman times; historical pigment
iron-rich metamorphic slate with warm red-brown tones; a natural earth pigment ground from sedimentary rock; historical pigment
nephrite or jadeite silicate mineral; revered across Chinese, Mesoamerican, and Maori cultures; the pale grey-green of unworked nephrite; historical pigment
calcium carbonate from ground eggshell; used in Japanese nihonga painting (gofun) and as a white pigment in manuscript illumination since the medieval period; historical pigment
lime white made from aged slaked lime mixed with crushed marble; described by Cennini in Il Libro dell'Arte (~1390) as the proper white for fresco painting; historical pigment
chromium-rich variety of muscovite mica; named for German mineralogist Johann Nepomuk von Fuchs; a shimmering pale green used as a decorative pigment; historical pigment
potassium iron sulfate hydroxide; named for the Jaroso ravine in Spain; a yellow-brown mineral identified in cave paintings and on the surface of Mars by the Opportunity rover; historical pigment
iron oxyhydroxide earth with a deep amber-gold tone; richer and more saturated than standard yellow ochre; a warm glazing earth; historical pigment
pale warm iron oxide earth; a buff-toned natural ochre used in flesh tones and architectural washes; historical pigment
eosin-based synthetic dye lake; bright peach-red; introduced in the late 19th c. as a cheaper alternative to natural madder lakes; historical pigment
